content.problemStatement:
Coach Chef has selected all the players and now he has to separate them into 2 teams, AAA and BBB.


Each player must be included in **exactly one** of the 2 teams and each player xxx, has a skill level SxS_xSx​. It is **not** necessary for both teams to have equal number of players, but **they have to be non-empty**.


Since the number of players is way too high, Chef doesn't have an actual list of every player individually. Instead, he keeps a list of NNN pairs {Xi,Yi}\{ X_i, Y_i \}{Xi​,Yi​}, which tells him that there are YiY_iYi​ players with skill level XiX_iXi​.



Chef thinks that a division into 2 teams is Good, if the skill value of **every** player in A divides the skill value of **every** player in B. More formally, Sb mod Sa=0S_b \bmod S_a = 0Sb​modSa​=0, ∀ a ∈ A, b ∈ B.



Since he is busy, can you help him find total number of Good divisions? Since the answer can be very large, print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.



**NOTE :** 2 divisions are considered different if there exists any player xxx belonging to different teams in the 2 divisions. (See example for more clarity).
constraints: Sum of $N$ over all testcases does not exceed $10^{5}$
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer $T$ denoting the number of test cases. The description of
        $T$ test cases follows.
    The first line for each testcase has a single integer $N$, denoting the number of pairs in Chef's list. 
    $N$ lines follow. Each contains 2 space separated integers $X_i$ and $Y_i$, denoting Chef has $Y_i$ players with skill level $X_i$.
